explanation.text: Paragraph 2 introduces the idea that important brain development occurs while teenagers sleep. The article develops it through research results because studies and specialists explain sleep's effects on attention, mental health, immunity, and brain maturation.

The quotation marks signal that screen activities only seem relaxing; their blue light makes the brain respond as if it were daytime and delays sleep. This irony suggests that some supposed relaxation actually prevents true rest.

The section explains that busy schedules, screen light, online emotions, caffeine, and a shifted circadian rhythm all delay teenagers' sleep. By linking those causes to later bedtimes and less sleep, it develops an important cause-and-effect relationship.

A major part of the article explains the broad consequences of insufficient sleep for teenagers' learning, health, and development. Difficulty paying attention belongs in a summary because it is one of those central effects, unlike the narrower supporting details in the other choices.

The mental-health claim is followed by research showing an inverse relationship between sleep and depression or other mental illnesses. As teenagers' sleep decreased, those risks increased, which provides direct evidence for the claim.
Calling teenage sleep loss "a serious public health problem" is an evaluation rather than a neutral fact. This wording reveals the author's view that the issue is important and harmful to society.

Paragraph 20 acknowledges unanswered research questions but states the clear conclusion that teenagers' brains need sleep. It best summarizes the central idea because it gives the article's overall finding rather than one cause or recommendation.

Ohkwa'ri pauses while Big Tree gives him time to "collect his thoughts" before he makes his request about building a lodge. The phrase means he is organizing his ideas because he is preparing to say something important.

At a forest stream, bending down to drink could let an enemy or dangerous animal approach unseen, while deeper water must be reached safely. These hazards require a person to stay watchful and carefully use a cup.

The sumac blowpipe is kept in plain sight because it can help more than one person, allowing anyone in the village to borrow and return it. This custom develops the idea that sharing useful objects benefits the whole community.

Ohkwa'ri says that building and sometimes sleeping in his own lodge would help him learn and become tougher and stronger. This goal supports the story's central idea about preparing for adulthood and greater responsibility.

Most of the story first shows Ohkwa'ri helping Big Tree make and finish a carefully crafted drinking cup. That shared work is important to a summary because it establishes their relationship and the lesson about becoming capable and mature.

Before paragraph 15, the story focuses on making the cup and explaining community customs. The plot changes direction because Ohkwa'ri's announcement that he wants to build a lodge introduces his readiness to grow up and live independently.

The narration reveals Ohkwa'ri's private thoughts about the skills he lacks, his boy's cup, and his plan to prepare for living on his own. These thoughts develop his point of view by showing how strongly he wants to become capable and mature.

Paragraph 2 says the monument would strengthen the bond between France and America and declare France's devotion to liberty and equality after Napoleon III. It contributes to the central idea because it explains both purposes behind the proposed gift.
Sowing a seed is the beginning of later growth. The metaphor refers to the project's first ideas because the discussion at the party eventually developed into the Statue of Liberty plan.
Bartholdi traveled to learn how Americans felt about the project and tried to increase their enthusiasm. Thus, "drum up" means creating broad public interest and demand for the proposed monument.

Bedloe's Island stood in active New York Harbor, where the monument would attract attention and greet immigrants entering the country. Its busy harbor location therefore made it ideal for the statue's intended visibility and meaning.

Instead of relying only on ordinary donation appeals, the organizers held banquets and balls and donated the food and ballroom proceeds. These inventive fundraising events best support the description of their efforts as "creative."

Americans were initially curious but unwilling to help finance the statue. The Franco-American Union's inclusion of supporters from both countries and its work collecting donations shows that American attitudes shifted toward active support.

Bartholdi continued pursuing the monument through years of delay, war, weak American enthusiasm, and financial difficulty. His commitment was a major contribution because it kept the idea alive until the project could attract support and begin construction.
